text: To differentiate between the two types of tumours, the plunging test is applied.
The rigidus scope is advanced very close to the tumour and the anaesthetist is asked to blow hard on the venturi insufflator.
If the tumour is seen to slide back and forth over normal looking mucosa, then a plunging tumour is likely.
Here is an example of a plunging tumour requiring bronchoplastic right upper lobectomy.

There are two important plunging signs.

The ferris is the plunging test by which the anaesthetist insuffilates the venturi device in the face of the tumour, forcing it to move backwards and forwards, allowing momentary scrutiny of the surrounding mucosal wall.
If the tumour is arising from the side walls, no such movement will be observed.
The second sign is the tonsure sign.
The tonsure sign mimics the monk's tonsure.
It represents a halo of pus around the tumour head.
If the halo is complete, that means all sides around the tumour are free.

Lord Brock in 1937 described the middle lobe syndrome in children with middle lobe collapse due to external compression by enlarged tuberculous lymph nodes.
